title: Parallel synthesis with fluorous reagents and reactants
abstract:
Parallel Synthesis with Fluorous
Reagents and Reactants
Dennis P. Curran
Department of Chemistry, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA 15260
M
Abstract: Fluorous reagents and reactants provide emerging new options in parallel synthesis be-
cause they are easily prepared, reacted, and then separated from the desired reaction products.
Some background information on fluorous reaction and separation techniques is provided, and the
utility of fluorous reagents and reactants is illustrated with a number of case studies that feature
both liquid-liquid and solid-solid separations. © 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Med Res Rev, 19, No. 5,
432–438, 1999.
Key words: flourous; parallel synthesis; flourocarbon bonded phase; phase switch 
1. I N T R O D U C T I O N
The advent of combinatorial chemistry and parallel synthesis1 has exposed gaps in currently avail-
able organic synthesis methodology. Among these, perhaps the two most important are the need for
more efficient and more general methodology, and the need to couple synthesis and separation. Im-
provements in synthetic methodology have been an enduring theme in synthesis research for some
time, but the area of purification has long been perceived as an independent field, not directly relat-
ed to synthesis. However, over the last few years, barriers between synthesis and separation have
started to crumble as synthetic chemists have realized that a synthetic step includes reaction, sepa-
ration, and identification/analysis stages, and that all of these stages must be optimized for rapid par-
allel synthesis.2
In a recent overview,2a we have posited the following goal for reaction design: “the product of
the reaction mixture should be allied to a different phase from everything else that is present in the
final reaction mixture.” To meet this goal requires the union of synthesis and separation into a sin-
gle strategy for reaction design and execution. The problem that we face is that most molecules tra-
ditionally used in synthesis are small, organic-soluble molecules, and such molecules are usually
separated from each other not by workup but by chromatography. The solution to the problem is to
make use of molecules that partition into other phases like the water (or ionic) phase, the solid phase,
the fluorous phase, or others. Many different techniques and reagents have emerged over the last few
years,3 and while these techniques are very different from the standpoint of execution, we have made
the case that they can be grouped together into a simple conceptual framework.2a
Contract grant sponsor: National Institutes of Health
Contract grant sponsor: National Science Foundation
432
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0198-6325/99/050432-07
 2. T H E  F L U O R O U S  P H A S E
Our work in this area has focused on the use of the “fluorous” phase4 as a complement to the more
commonly employed phases in organic synthesis. Fluorocarbons and other highly fluorinated liquids
are now coming to be called “fluorous solvents.” The rechristening of an old class of compounds
with a new name is not an idle exercise—the name “fluorous” nicely emphasizes that this class of
highly fluorinated liquids forms a phase unto itself. Fluorous solvents are always immiscible in wa-
ter and are frequently immiscible in organic solvents as well. Accordingly, two- and three-phase ex-
tractions can readily be conducted and molecules bearing a sufficient number of fluorines will par-
tition into the fluorous liquid phase. Typical organic compounds are insoluble in fluorous solvents,
so multiple extractions can be conducted to separate the fluorous and organic reaction components.
Not surprisingly, there is a trade off at the reaction stage and the separation stage in the number of
fluorines contained in a fluorous molecule. Molecules with very large numbers of fluorines have little
or no solubility in traditional organic solvents. Such molecules are useful in the techniques of “fluorous
biphasic catalysis”4 because certain organic/fluorous solvent pairs can be made miscible by heating.
Thus, reactions are conducted under homogeneous conditions at higher temperature and separations are
conducted after cooling to reform two phases. Such reactions can be ideal for catalytic processes, but
are somewhat limited in general due to solvent restrictions (especially because fluorous solvents are very
nonpolar). A more general approach is to use a lightly fluorinated reaction solvent (or cosolvent) that
dissolves both organic and fluorous reaction components, and then  subsequently induce partitioning of
the homogeneous reaction mixture into organic and fluorous phases after the reaction. Useful lightly flu-
orinated solvents include benzotrifluoride (BTF),5 Cl2FCCF2Cl, and trifluoroethanol, among others.
In the trade off between homogeneous reaction conditions and phase separation at the workup
stage, the liquid–liquid extraction method begins to break down as the number of fluorines on the
fluorous molecule is decreased. This is especially true for fluorous-tagged molecules with large and/
or polar organic components. Fortunately, a solid–liquid extraction procedure comes to the rescue
in such cases.6 Reaction mixtures are eluted in two stages through “fluorous reverse phase silica gel”
(silica gel with a fluorocarbon bonded phase). This silica gel retains molecules primarily based on
fluorine content, and reaction mixtures containing fluorous and organic products can often be worked
up by a simple two-stage filtration. After charging the mixture to the column, an elution with a weak-
er mobile phase provides an organic fraction. A second elution with a stronger mobile phase then
provides a fluorous fraction. These solid phase extractions are readily conducted in parallel and the
resulting liquid fractions are concentrated on a vacuum centrifuge prior to automated analysis.
The following section provides a brief overview of some of the fluorous techniques that we have
introduced with an emphasis on parallel synthesis with fluorous reagents. This is only a small subset of
the kinds of fluorous techniques that are becoming available. Many of the techniques are highly suited
for “green chemistry” applications, although this feature will not be emphasized here. Experimental de-
tails for many of these reactions can be found in the cited articles, and a chapter focusing specifically on
the practical and technical aspects of these experiments is also in press.7 Much useful practical infor-
mation about fluorous solvents and ligands can also be found in an article by Gladysz and Barthel-Rosa.8
3. E X A M P L E S
Our first foray into parallel synthesis was the construction of a small library of radical addition prod-
ucts, as shown in Figure 1.9 Three alkyl halides and three electron poor alkenes were reacted under
standard catalytic condition for the Giese reaction, except that the usual reagent tributyltin hydride
was replaced with the indicated fluorous tin hydride. Each of the reactions was subjected to simple
three-phase liquid extraction and evaporation to provide nine organic products in good to excellent
yield. This library provides a very basic example for separation strategy and phase planning at work.
The reaction conditions were chosen to provide a high yield based on the limiting alkyl halide pre-

2. T H E  F L U O R O U S  P H A S E
Our work in this area has focused on the use of the “fluorous” phase4 as a complement to the more
commonly employed phases in organic synthesis. Fluorocarbons and other highly fluorinated liquids
are now coming to be called “fluorous solvents.” The rechristening of an old class of compounds
with a new name is not an idle exercise—the name “fluorous” nicely emphasizes that this class of
highly fluorinated liquids forms a phase unto itself. Fluorous solvents are always immiscible in wa-
ter and are frequently immiscible in organic solvents as well. Accordingly, two- and three-phase ex-
tractions can readily be conducted and molecules bearing a sufficient number of fluorines will par-
tition into the fluorous liquid phase. Typical organic compounds are insoluble in fluorous solvents,
so multiple extractions can be conducted to separate the fluorous and organic reaction components.
Not surprisingly, there is a trade off at the reaction stage and the separation stage in the number of
fluorines contained in a fluorous molecule. Molecules with very large numbers of fluorines have little
or no solubility in traditional organic solvents. Such molecules are useful in the techniques of “fluorous
biphasic catalysis”4 because certain organic/fluorous solvent pairs can be made miscible by heating.
Thus, reactions are conducted under homogeneous conditions at higher temperature and separations are
conducted after cooling to reform two phases. Such reactions can be ideal for catalytic processes, but
are somewhat limited in general due to solvent restrictions (especially because fluorous solvents are very
nonpolar). A more general approach is to use a lightly fluorinated reaction solvent (or cosolvent) that
dissolves both organic and fluorous reaction components, and then  subsequently induce partitioning of
the homogeneous reaction mixture into organic and fluorous phases after the reaction. Useful lightly flu-
orinated solvents include benzotrifluoride (BTF),5 Cl2FCCF2Cl, and trifluoroethanol, among others.
In the trade off between homogeneous reaction conditions and phase separation at the workup
stage, the liquid–liquid extraction method begins to break down as the number of fluorines on the
fluorous molecule is decreased. This is especially true for fluorous-tagged molecules with large and/
or polar organic components. Fortunately, a solid–liquid extraction procedure comes to the rescue
in such cases.6 Reaction mixtures are eluted in two stages through “fluorous reverse phase silica gel”
(silica gel with a fluorocarbon bonded phase). This silica gel retains molecules primarily based on
fluorine content, and reaction mixtures containing fluorous and organic products can often be worked
up by a simple two-stage filtration. After charging the mixture to the column, an elution with a weak-
er mobile phase provides an organic fraction. A second elution with a stronger mobile phase then
provides a fluorous fraction. These solid phase extractions are readily conducted in parallel and the
resulting liquid fractions are concentrated on a vacuum centrifuge prior to automated analysis.
The following section provides a brief overview of some of the fluorous techniques that we have
introduced with an emphasis on parallel synthesis with fluorous reagents. This is only a small subset of
the kinds of fluorous techniques that are becoming available. Many of the techniques are highly suited
for “green chemistry” applications, although this feature will not be emphasized here. Experimental de-
tails for many of these reactions can be found in the cited articles, and a chapter focusing specifically on
the practical and technical aspects of these experiments is also in press.7 Much useful practical infor-
mation about fluorous solvents and ligands can also be found in an article by Gladysz and Barthel-Rosa.8
Our first foray into parallel synthesis was the construction of a small library of radical addition prod-
ucts, as shown in Figure 1.9 Three alkyl halides and three electron poor alkenes were reacted under
standard catalytic condition for the Giese reaction, except that the usual reagent tributyltin hydride
was replaced with the indicated fluorous tin hydride. Each of the reactions was subjected to simple
three-phase liquid extraction and evaporation to provide nine organic products in good to excellent
yield. This library provides a very basic example for separation strategy and phase planning at work.
The reaction conditions were chosen to provide a high yield based on the limiting alkyl halide pre-
FLUOROUS REAGENTS AND REACTANTS
cursors. Ideally (and also in practice), the product derived from this is the only thing left in the or-
ganic liquid phase after the workup. The inorganic products are removed in the water phase, the flu-
orous tin hydride is removed in the fluorous phase (and is recovered for reuse) and the excess alkene
is evaporated into the gas phase. If desired, nonvolatile alkenes can also be removed by a phase
switch. Excess tin hydride is added after the reaction to hydrostannate the unreacted alkene and the
hydrostannated product then partitions into the fluorous phase with the tin hydride. Recently, we have
introduced a whole family of tin hydrides descending from the parent in Figure 1.9e These have dif-
ferent spacers, different lengths of fluorinated chains, and different numbers of chains. The result-
ing family of reagents provides a range of options for tin hydride chemistry.
In the tin hydride library, the fluorous component serves as a reagent. That is, it promotes the
coupling of the reactants but does not contribute to the diversity of the resulting library. However,
fluorous reactants are also useful tools. For example, we have coupled a series of fluorous aryl stan-
nanes with aryl and benzyl halides and triflates under standard conditions (lithium chloride is es-
sential for good yields), followed by three phase extraction (Fig. 2).10 This provides the coupled
products in the organic phase along with the fluorous tin chloride in the fluorous phase.
While the separation chemistry in the original Stille coupling method worked very well, there
were two problems with the reaction end of the process. First, the reaction was very slow (1–2 days),
and second, the organic products were contaminated with 5–10% of the homocoupled product from
the stannane precursor (a common problem in Stille couplings). Both problems were solved by mi-
crowave irradiation in experiments conducted by Prof. Anders Hallberg and Dr. Mats Larhed in Up-
psala, Sweden.11 Reaction times were reduced to 1–2 min, and hetereocoupled products of high pu-
rity were isolated. The use of microwaves is very promising for fluorous techniques as well as for
other parallel synthesis techniques.
We have also made a number of libraries based on allylation reactions with fluorous allyl stan-
nanes, and one of these is illustrated in Figure 3.12 Typical radical, ionic, and transition metal reac-
Figure 1. Parallel Giese reactions with a fluorous tin hydride.
Figure 2. Fluorous Stille couplings.
FLUOROUS REAGENTS AND REACTANTS
tions did not work well with fluorous allyl stannanes having “ethylene” spacers, so we used instead
the new “propylene” spacer reagents shown. In general, such propylene spacer reagents are more
soluble in organic solvents than their lower homologs. While this is good for the reaction stage of a
process, it means that a large number (5–10 or more) of liquid–liquid extractions are needed to free
the organic products from the fluorous tin residues in the separation stage. Accordingly, the fluorous
solid phase extraction is the preferred method for workup of these parallel reactions.
Unfortunately, the data in Figure 3 are not quite as good as they look at first glance. While the
yields of allylated products (against an internal standard) are fine, the products are not pure. This is
because the catalyst is organic, and it passes throughout the fluorous silica gel with the organic prod-
ucts. Indeed, small signals from the phosphine ligand are readily detected in the proton NMR spec-
tra of all the products. To solve this problem, we have recently prepared a fluorous catalyst, and in-
deed this catalyzes the reaction but can be retained on the fluorous silica gel during the first elution.13
The use of “phase switches” is a powerful technique for coupling synthesis and separation.2a Gen-
erally speaking, two or more small organic reaction components are mixed and allowed to react. After
the reaction, a “phase tagging” reagent is added to selectively switch the phase of one or more products.
In this approach, separation is dictated by a chemoselective reaction. “Quenching” of excess reagents
or reactants has rapidly become one of the most popular techniques due to its simplicity, and procedures
based on both solid phase quenching and tagging with ionizable groups have been vigorously devel-
oped.3 These methods rely on filtration-based separations, and problems arise whenever the desired (or-
ganic) product precipitates from the reaction mixture. In the synthesis of large libraries, this can be a
common occurrence in some classes of molecules. Fluorous quenching allied with the liquid–liquid ex-
tractive workup provides a general method that deals with both soluble and insoluble reaction products.
In introducing ourselves to robotic methods, we have synthesized a number of urea libraries with
the aid of fluorous amine quenching. The results from one of these libraries are shown in Figure 4 and
Table I.14 Limiting amounts of three amines were coupled with modest excesses (1.5 equiv) of three iso-
cyanates. After a suitable coupling time, the reaction mixtures were “quenched” with a highly fluorous
(78 fluorines) amine and allowed to stand for a few hours so that reaction of the amine with the excess
isocyanate was complete. Robotic liquid–liquid extraction then provided the pure organic ureas in the
organic phase (THF), while the fluorous ureas and the unreacted amine quencher partitioned into the flu-
orous phase. In a few cases, the urea products precipitated from the reactions, but these products are com-
pletely “fluorophobic” and remained suspended in the organic phase during the extraction procedure.
Figure 3. Parallel allylations with a fluorous allyl stannane.
Thus, the unreacted isocyanate was removed and the residue from evaporation of the organic phase con-
tained the desired urea independent of whether the urea was soluble or insoluble in the reaction medium.
The data in Table I also shows that the fluorine content of the desired products is very low
(,0.5%), as assessed by fluorine NMR. The addition of fluorinated reactants to the library is a tech-
nique that we now commonly use to assess the efficiency of such quenching procedures. The fluo-
rines in the product provide an internal standard against which to assess fluorous impurities.
4. C O N C L U S I O N S  A N D  F U T U R E  D I R E C T I O N S
Parallel and combinatorial synthesis with fluorous techniques is still a relatively small and under de-
veloped area. But the early results show excellent promise for future application. The techniques are
generally complementary to existing methods based on solid phase reactions or acid-base chemistry,
aWaters Nova-Pack®C18 3.9 3 150 mm. Flow rate 1.5 mL/min. Detection at 210
nm. solvent: acetonitrile 40 to 60% in parenthesis refer to the amount of symmet-
bMol-%, determined in the 19F-NMR spectrum, relative to the product.
Figure 4. Urea synthesis with fluorous amine quenching.
FLUOROUS REAGENTS AND REACTANTS
and therefore offer new options or solve problems in different ways. As our experience in fluorous
chemistry increases, so will its utility in parallel synthesis as well as in other areas.
Of special importance to the parallel synthesis community are fluorous synthesis techniques
where the substrates and products, as opposed to the reactants and reagents, are labeled. The con-
cepts and groundwork for these techniques have already been laid,15 but the current published ap-
plications based on liquid–liquid extractions are of limited use because many fluorines are needed
on a tag to induce a large organic molecule to partition into a fluorinated liquid. We will soon report
important breakthroughs in solid phase extraction techniques which allow separation of large organic
molecules bearing tags with many fewer fluorines than those employed to date.
I warmly thank the talented and motivated group of coworkers who have driven our work in this area; their names can be found
